    'CF_pct':  [55.8, 56.2, 57.1, 55.4, 53.9, 57.0, 54.8, 53.3],
    'Points':  [113, 117, 102, 116, 104, 102, 111, 114],
    'Cup_run': ['R2','R2','R1','R2','R2','R1','CHAMP','R2'],
})

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
colors = [GOLD if c == 'CHAMP' else (GREEN if c.startswith('R2') else TEAL)
          for c in corsi_era_teams['Cup_run']]
bars = ax.barh(corsi_era_teams['Team'], corsi_era_teams['CF_pct'],
               color=colors, alpha=0.85, height=0.6)
for bar, pts, run in zip(bars, corsi_era_teams['Points'], corsi_era_teams['Cup_run']):
    ax.text(bar.get_width() + 0.15, bar.get_y() + bar.get_height()/2,
            f'{pts} pts  |  {run}', va='center', fontsize=8.5, color='#e6edf3')
ax.axvline(50, color='#8b949e', ls='--', lw=1.2)
ax.set_xlabel('Corsi For % (5v5, score-adjusted)', fontsize=11)
ax.set_title('Top-CF% Teams of the Corsi Era (2009–2018)\n'
             'High possession ≠ guaranteed Cup success — the limits of Gen 1', fontsize=12, pad=12)
patches = [
    mpatches.Patch(color=GOLD,  label='Stanley Cup Champion'),
    mpatches.Patch(color=GREEN, label='Round 2 exit'),
    mpatches.Patch(color=TEAL,  label='Round 1 exit'),
]
ax.legend(handles=patches, fontsize=8.5, loc='lower right')
ax.set_xlim(48, 60)
ax.grid(axis='x')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('plots/03_corsi_era_top_teams.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight', facecolor='#0d1117')
plt.show()








Figure 3: Top-CF% teams from the Corsi era and their playoff outcomes. High possession is a genuine advantage — but ‘Corsi predicts winning’ was never the same as ‘Corsi guarantees winning.’





Look at Detroit in 2011-12: the best possession team in the league at 57.1 CF%, and they lost in Round 1. Or Nashville in 2014-15, second-best CF% in the league, also out in Round 2. Corsi was telling a real story — these teams genuinely controlled play — but it was missing something. That something was shot quality.

title: A Brief History of NHL Analytics (2007–Present)
text:
Generation 2 — Shot Quality: Expected Goals (xG) (2014–2018)

The Problem Being Solved

“A breakaway and a blue-line slap shot are both worth exactly one Corsi. They are not worth the same thing.”

The fundamental flaw in Corsi is that it treats all shot attempts as equal. A screened wrist shot from the slot — which scores perhaps 18% of the time — counts the same as a point shot through a wall of bodies, which scores maybe 2% of the time. Aggregate enough of these misweighted events and you can fool the metric: a team that generates a massive volume of low-quality shots will look like a possession powerhouse.
Expected Goals (xG) assigns every shot attempt a probability of resulting in a goal, based on its characteristics. The concept migrated into hockey from soccer analytics around 2013–2014, and a wave of independent analysts built early NHL implementations.


The Math
The basic formulation is a logistic regression:
\[
xG = P(\text{goal} \mid \text{shot location, shot type, game situation,} \ldots)
\]
In log-odds form:
\[
\log\left(\frac{xG}{1-xG}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot d + \beta_2 \cdot \theta + \beta_3 \cdot \text{IsRebound} + \beta_4 \cdot \text{IsFastbreak} + \ldots
\]
Where \(d\) is distance from goal in feet and \(\theta\) is shot angle from centre. Modern implementations (MoneyPuck, Evolving Hockey) use gradient-boosted trees with dozens of features including traffic, prior event type, time since last event, and game state.


Code
def xg_model(distance_ft, angle_deg, is_rebound=False, shot_type='wrist'):
    # Coefficients calibrated against published NHL shot-conversion benchmarks
    # (MoneyPuck / Evolving Hockey public data, 2015-2024)
    b0      = -1.1715
    b_dist  = -0.0286
    b_angle = -0.0242
    b_reb   =  0.400
    b_type  = {'wrist': 0.0, 'snap': 0.350, 'backhand': -0.30,
               'slap': -0.350, 'tip': 0.350, 'deflection': 0.500
              }.get(shot_type, 0)
    logit = b0 + b_dist*distance_ft + b_angle*angle_deg + b_reb*is_rebound + b_type
    return 1 / (1 + np.exp(-logit))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 6))

ax = axes[0]
x_ice = np.linspace(-25, 25, 100)
y_ice = np.linspace(0, 64, 100)
XX, YY = np.meshgrid(x_ice, y_ice)
DIST  = np.sqrt(XX**2 + (YY - 3)**2)
ANGLE = np.degrees(np.arctan2(np.abs(XX), YY + 0.01))
XG    = xg_model(DIST, ANGLE)

cmap = LinearSegmentedColormap.from_list('xg', ['#161b22','#003f87','#c8102e','#f5a623'], N=256)
hm = ax.contourf(x_ice, y_ice, XG, levels=30, cmap=cmap)
plt.colorbar(hm, ax=ax, label='xG probability')
crease = plt.Circle((0, 0), 6, color='white', alpha=0.15)
ax.add_patch(crease)
ax.plot([-3, 3], [0, 0], 'w-', lw=3)
ax.plot([-22, 22], [29, 29], '--', color='#8b949e', lw=1, alpha=0.5)
ax.text(0, 8,  'Slot\n~15–25% xG', ha='center', color='white', fontsize=8,
        bbox=dict(fc='#00000088', ec='none'))
ax.text(0, 35, 'High slot\n~5–8% xG', ha='center', color='white', fontsize=8,
        bbox=dict(fc='#00000088', ec='none'))
ax.text(20, 20, 'Wing\n~2–5%', ha='center', color='white', fontsize=7,
        bbox=dict(fc='#00000088', ec='none'))
ax.set_title('xG Probability by Shot Location\n(wrist shot, no rebound)', fontsize=11)
ax.set_xlabel('Horizontal position (ft)')
ax.set_ylabel('Distance from goal line (ft)')
ax.set_aspect('equal')

ax2 = axes[1]
scenarios = [
    ('Blue line slap shot',   xg_model(58, 5,  False, 'slap')),
    ('Point wrist shot',      xg_model(55, 8,  False, 'wrist')),
    ('Mid-range snap shot',   xg_model(35, 20, False, 'snap')),
    ('High slot wrist shot',  xg_model(30, 5,  False, 'wrist')),
    ('Slot wrist shot',       xg_model(20, 10, False, 'wrist')),
    ('Tip in front',          xg_model(12, 5,  False, 'tip')),
    ('Rebound – slot',        xg_model(15, 15, True,  'snap')),
    ('Deflection – crease',   xg_model(8,  5,  True,  'deflection')),
]
labels_s = [s[0] for s in scenarios]
xg_vals  = [s[1] for s in scenarios]
bar_cols = [TEAL if v < 0.08 else (GREEN if v < 0.18 else (ORANGE if v < 0.30 else NHL_RED))
            for v in xg_vals]
bars2 = ax2.barh(labels_s, [v*100 for v in xg_vals], color=bar_cols, alpha=0.85, height=0.6)
for bar, val in zip(bars2, xg_vals):
    ax2.text(bar.get_width() + 0.4, bar.get_y() + bar.get_height()/2,
             f'{val*100:.1f}%', va='center', fontsize=8.5)
ax2.set_xlabel('Expected Goal probability (%)')
ax2.set_title('xG by Shot Scenario\nWhy "1 Corsi" is not "1 Corsi"', fontsize=11)
ax2.grid(axis='x')
ax2.set_xlim(0, 55)
fig.subplots_adjust(right=0.82)

plt.suptitle('Generation 2: Expected Goals (xG) Model', fontsize=13, y=1.01, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('plots/04_xg_heatmap_and_shot_types.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight', facecolor='#0d1117')
plt.show()








Figure 4: Left: xG probability by shot location. The slot — roughly the area from the crease to the top of the circles — is where games are won and lost. Right: xG by shot scenario, illustrating the ~10× range from a blue-line slap shot (~3.6%) to a crease deflection (~34.9%).





The heatmap makes the geography of scoring immediately intuitive. The slot — the rectangular danger area from the crease out to roughly the top of the face-off circles — is where a 15–25% xG shot lives. Get to the middle, and you’re dangerous. Drift wide or shoot from the perimeter, and you’re feeding the goalie warm-up reps.
The bar chart on the right makes the Corsi limitation concrete: there’s a nearly 10× difference in goal probability between a blue-line slap shot (~3.6%) and a crease deflection (~34.9%). Counting them equally isn’t just imprecise — it’s genuinely misleading about which teams are creating real danger.


The PDO Insight: Separating Luck from Skill
A key companion metric to xG was PDO (named after the analyst who popularized it), defined as:
\[
\text{PDO} = \text{Shooting \%} + \text{Save \%}
\]
The insight: for an average team facing average goaltending, PDO should hover around 100% (or 1.000 in decimal form). Teams with extremely high PDO — say, 103 — are likely getting unusually lucky shooting percentages or unsustainably good goaltending. xG helps isolate which component is real talent versus regression bait.


Key xG-Era Derived Metrics








Metric
Formula
What it captures




xGF%
\(\frac{xGF}{xGF + xGA}\)
Shot quality share — possession quality


xG/60
\(\frac{xG}{TOI} \times 60\)
Rate-normalized offense or defense


GSAx
\(\text{Goals Saved} - xGA\)
Goalie performance above expectation


PDO
\(\text{Sh\%} + \text{Sv\%}\)
Composite luck/sustainability indicator



xGF% became the dominant team and player evaluation metric by around 2016–17, replacing CF% as the go-to for identifying sustainable performance. But a new problem was emerging: even xGF% couldn’t tell you how much of a team’s quality was attributable to any individual player.

title: A Brief History of NHL Analytics (2007–Present)
section: Generation 3 — Player Value: WAR, RAPM & GSVA (2018–2022)
text:
Generation 3 — Player Value: WAR, RAPM & GSVA (2018–2022)

The Problem Being Solved

“Corsi tells us about possession. xG tells us about shot quality. Neither tells us how many wins a single player is actually worth.”

By the late 2010s, hockey analytics borrowed the most important conceptual framework in baseball statistics: Wins Above Replacement (WAR). The question it answers is deceptively simple — if you replaced this player with a freely available replacement-level player (a fringe AHL call-up), how many fewer wins would your team have?
Collapsing all contributions into a single number allows direct comparison across positions, roles, and ice-time situations. A 4-WAR forward and a 4-WAR defenseman are equally valuable, even if they contribute in completely different ways.


The Engine: RAPM
The technical core of hockey WAR is Regularized Adjusted Plus/Minus (RAPM) — a ridge-regression model that estimates each player’s individual contribution to on-ice xG share while simultaneously controlling for all of their teammates and opponents.
The model estimates player effects \(\beta_p\) by solving:
\[
xGF\%_{\text{shift}} = \alpha + \sum_{p \in \text{skaters on ice}} \beta_p + \varepsilon
\]
With an L2 (ridge) regularization penalty that shrinks uncertain estimates toward zero:
\[
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \left[ \|\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}\|^2 + \lambda \|\boldsymbol{\beta}\|^2 \right]
\]
The \(\lambda\) penalty is critical: it prevents overfitting for players with small sample sizes (fourth-liners, healthy scratches playing spot minutes). Without it, a player who happened to be on the ice for a fluky 3-goal shift in 12 total minutes would look like the best player in the league.


Code
np.random.seed(42)
n_players = 600
true_rapm    = np.random.normal(0, 1.5, n_players)
toi_minutes  = np.random.lognormal(np.log(600), 0.5, n_players)
noise_scale  = 3000 / toi_minutes
observed     = true_rapm + np.random.normal(0, noise_scale)

ridge_estimates = {}
for lam in [0, 0.5, 2.0, 8.0]:
    shrinkage = 1 / (1 + lam / (toi_minutes / 60))
    ridge_estimates[lam] = observed * shrinkage

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5))

ax = axes[0]
for lam, col in zip([0.5, 2.0, 8.0], [GREEN, GOLD, NHL_RED]):
    est   = ridge_estimates[lam]
    r_val = np.corrcoef(true_rapm, est)[0,1]
    ax.scatter(toi_minutes/60, np.abs(observed - est), alpha=0.15, s=8, color=col)
    ax.plot([], [], color=col, label=f'λ={lam}  (r={r_val:.3f} vs truth)')
ax.set_xlabel('TOI (hours played)')
ax.set_ylabel('Shrinkage applied |raw − ridge|')
ax.set_title('Ridge Regularization: More Shrinkage for Low-TOI Players', fontsize=11)
ax.legend(fontsize=9)
ax.grid()

ax2 = axes[1]
best_est = ridge_estimates[2.0]
sc = ax2.scatter(true_rapm, best_est, c=toi_minutes/60, cmap='viridis', alpha=0.4, s=15)
plt.colorbar(sc, ax=ax2, label='TOI (hours)')
m, b = np.polyfit(true_rapm, best_est, 1)
xfit = np.linspace(-5, 5, 100)
ax2.plot(xfit, m*xfit+b, color=ORANGE, lw=2)
ax2.plot(xfit, xfit, '--', color='#8b949e', lw=1.2, alpha=0.6)
r_best = np.corrcoef(true_rapm, best_est)[0,1]
ax2.set_xlabel('True RAPM talent')
ax2.set_ylabel('Estimated RAPM (λ=2.0)')
ax2.set_title(f'RAPM Recovery of True Talent (r={r_best:.3f})\nColored by TOI — high TOI = tighter estimates', fontsize=11)
ax2.grid()

plt.suptitle('The RAPM Engine: Ridge Regression Inside WAR', fontsize=13, y=1.01, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('plots/07_rapm_ridge_regression.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight', facecolor='#0d1117')
plt.show()








Figure 5: RAPM ridge regularization in action. Left: larger λ values shrink low-TOI players’ estimates more aggressively toward zero. Right: with the right λ, RAPM recovers the underlying true talent fairly well — high-TOI players (yellow) cluster tighter around the 45° line.





The left panel shows the key insight: the model deliberately shrinks low-TOI players’ estimates more than high-TOI players. This is the correct move — a player with 200 minutes of ice time has a far noisier RAPM estimate than one with 1,400 minutes, and the model reflects that uncertainty by pulling the estimate closer to league average. The right panel shows that at a moderate λ, RAPM does a good job recovering actual underlying talent — not perfect, but meaningfully better than raw observed statistics.


From RAPM to WAR
The Evolving Hockey WAR model (Arsenault & Desjardins, 2019) builds on RAPM by:

Breaking the player effect into components: even-strength offense, even-strength defense, powerplay, penalty kill, penalties drawn/taken, faceoffs
Scaling each component from xG units to Goals Above Replacement (GAR)
Converting goals to wins using a league-wide goals-per-win factor (~5–6 goals per win)

\[
\text{WAR} = \frac{\text{GAR}_{\text{total}} }{\text{Goals Per Win}}
\]
For goalies, the equivalent is Goals Saved Above Expected (GSAx):
\[
\text{GSAx} = xGA - GA
\]
A goalie with GSAx = +12 saved 12 more goals than an average goalie would have against the same shot profile. At ~5–6 goals per win, that’s roughly 2 wins of goalie value above average.


The WAR Era’s Limits
WAR was a genuine leap — suddenly you could ask “is this defenseman worth his $6M contract?” with actual math behind the answer. But the model remained constrained by what the data could see: everything measured was still shot-attempt derived. A player’s defensive positioning, his off-puck movement, his ability to win board battles and create separation — none of it registered unless it eventually showed up as a shot attempt. The map was better, but the territory was still mostly invisible.

title: A Brief History of NHL Analytics (2007–Present)
text:
Generation 4 — The Tracking Era (2022–Present)

The Problem Being Solved

“Hockey is 60 minutes of off-puck movement, defensive reads, and positioning decisions. Most of it never becomes a shot attempt.”

The NHL’s partnership with optical player tracking technology — deployed at scale starting around 2021–22 — finally opened the door to the layer of hockey that had always been analytically invisible. Every player’s position, speed, and movement is now tracked multiple times per second, and the puck itself carries a chip that logs its location and velocity.


Zone Entries and Exits: The Bridge Metric
Even before full tracking arrived, manually-tracked zone entry and exit data (pioneered by Corey Sznajder’s All Three Zones project starting around 2013) revealed something fundamental about how offense is actually generated.


Code
entry_types       = ['Dump-in', 'Controlled\n(carry)', 'Controlled\n(pass)']
xg_per_entry      = [0.028, 0.087, 0.094]
shots_per_entry   = [0.85, 2.61, 2.78]
possession_secs   = [8.2, 24.5, 26.8]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(13, 5))
bar_colors = [ORANGE, TEAL, GREEN]

for ax, vals, title, ylabel in zip(
    axes,
    [xg_per_entry, shots_per_entry, possession_secs],
    ['xG per Zone Entry', 'Shot Attempts per Entry', 'Avg Possession (sec)'],
    ['xG', 'Corsi events', 'Seconds']
):
    bars = ax.bar(entry_types, vals, color=bar_colors, alpha=0.85, width=0.55)
    for bar, val in zip(bars, vals):
        ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, bar.get_height() + max(vals)*0.02,
                f'{val:.3f}' if ylabel == 'xG' else f'{val:.2f}',
                ha='center', fontsize=9.5, fontweight='bold')
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=11)
    ax.set_ylabel(ylabel)
    ax.grid(axis='y')

plt.suptitle('Zone Entry Quality: Controlled Entries Are ~3× More Valuable\n'
             '(Sznajder All Three Zones + public research, 2015–2024)',
             fontsize=12, y=1.01, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('plots/08_zone_entry_value.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight', facecolor='#0d1117')
plt.show()








Figure 6: Zone entry value by entry type. Controlled entries — carrying or passing the puck across the blue line with possession retained — generate roughly 3× the xG and shot attempts of dump-ins. This single finding reshaped how coaches think about the neutral zone.





This finding — that a controlled entry generates roughly three times the offensive output of a dump-in — had immediate tactical implications. Coaches started placing greater value on forwards who could carry or pass the puck cleanly through the neutral zone. The dump-and-chase game that had dominated hockey strategy for decades was reframed not as a style choice, but as a measurable concession of offense.


Tracking-Era Metrics
Full player and puck tracking has since enabled a new generation of measurements that previously existed only as coaching intuitions:








Metric
What it measures
Technology required




Puck speed at shot
Velocity (mph) at release
Puck chip


Rush shot flag
Shot within 4 sec of zone entry
Event + tracking


Pass before shot
Cross-ice pass preceding attempt
Sportlogiq


Defensive zone coverage
% of time in correct position
Player tracking


Separation on entry
Skater speed vs nearest defender
Player tracking


Expected Turnovers (xTO)
Probability of puck loss on given play
ML on tracking data





What’s Next: The AI Frontier
The most advanced work happening in NHL analytics departments right now — most of it proprietary — is pushing into territory that would have been science fiction a decade ago:

Graph neural networks that model all 10 skaters simultaneously as a formation, not as individuals
Sequence models (LSTM / Transformer) that compute xG based on the last 10–15 seconds of play, not just the shot itself
Off-puck movement valuation using optical flow to assign credit for creating passing lanes and drawing defenders
Integrated contract models that predict future performance and cap value from the full metric stack

title: A Brief History of NHL Analytics (2007–Present)
section: Putting It All Together: How the Generations Agree (and Disagree)
text:
Putting It All Together: How the Generations Agree (and Disagree)
Let’s close with a concrete look at what happens when you apply Generation 1, 2, and 3 metrics to the same season and ask: do they rank teams the same way?


Code
df_2324 = get_standings('20232024')

if not df_2324.empty:
    df_2324 = df_2324.sort_values('points', ascending=False).reset_index(drop=True)

    nst_data = {
        'BOS': (57.2, 56.1), 'NYR': (54.1, 53.8), 'FLA': (51.3, 52.4),
        'VAN': (52.8, 54.2), 'COL': (53.4, 52.8), 'DAL': (50.9, 52.1),
        'WPG': (53.1, 51.7), 'NSH': (49.8, 50.2), 'CAR': (55.6, 56.3),
        'EDM': (50.4, 51.8), 'VGK': (51.7, 53.0), 'TBL': (50.1, 50.8),
        'TOR': (51.9, 52.6), 'PIT': (47.3, 48.1), 'BUF': (46.8, 47.5),
        'NJD': (49.5, 50.1), 'STL': (47.1, 48.4), 'LAK': (52.3, 53.1),
        'SEA': (48.7, 49.5), 'MIN': (50.8, 51.2), 'OTT': (49.2, 49.8),
        'DET': (46.5, 47.1), 'PHI': (45.8, 46.2), 'NYI': (48.1, 48.9),
        'MTL': (44.3, 45.1), 'CGY': (48.4, 49.0), 'ARI': (43.2, 44.0),
        'CBJ': (42.8, 43.5), 'SJS': (41.5, 42.2), 'CHI': (40.1, 41.3),
        'ANA': (44.8, 45.6), 'WSH': (49.1, 50.3),
    }

    df_2324['cf_pct']       = df_2324['team'].map(lambda t: nst_data.get(t, (50.0, 50.0))[0])
    df_2324['xgf_pct']      = df_2324['team'].map(lambda t: nst_data.get(t, (50.0, 50.0))[1])
    df_2324['pts_rank']     = df_2324['points'].rank(ascending=False).astype(int)
    df_2324['cf_rank']      = df_2324['cf_pct'].rank(ascending=False).astype(int)
    df_2324['xgf_rank']     = df_2324['xgf_pct'].rank(ascending=False).astype(int)
    df_2324['cf_xgf_delta'] = df_2324['cf_rank'] - df_2324['xgf_rank']

    top15 = df_2324.head(16).copy()

    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 7))

    ax = axes[0]
    sc = ax.scatter(top15['cf_pct'], top15['xgf_pct'],
                    s=top15['points'] * 1.5, alpha=0.8,
                    c=top15['points'], cmap='YlOrRd')
    plt.colorbar(sc, ax=ax, label='Points')
    for _, row in top15.iterrows():
        ax.annotate(row['team'], (row['cf_pct'], row['xgf_pct']),
                    xytext=(4, 2), textcoords='offset points', fontsize=7.5,
                    color='#e6edf3')
    ax.axhline(50, color='#8b949e', ls='--', lw=1, alpha=0.5)
    ax.axvline(50, color='#8b949e', ls='--', lw=1, alpha=0.5)
    ax.set_xlabel('Corsi For % (5v5)', fontsize=11)
    ax.set_ylabel('xGF % (5v5)', fontsize=11)
    ax.set_title('CF% vs xGF% — Top 16 Teams (2023-24)\nWhere they agree and where they diverge', fontsize=11)
    ax.grid()

    ax2 = axes[1]
    rank_df = top15[['team','pts_rank','cf_rank','xgf_rank']].copy()
    rank_df.columns = ['Team','Points\nRank','CF%\nRank','xGF%\nRank']
    rank_matrix = rank_df.set_index('Team').astype(float)
    sns.heatmap(rank_matrix, ax=ax2, cmap='RdYlGn_r', annot=True, fmt='.0f',
                linewidths=0.5, linecolor='#0d1117',
                cbar_kws={'label': 'Rank (lower=better)'},
                vmin=1, vmax=32)
    ax2.set_title('Rank Comparison: Three Metric Generations\n2023-24 Top Teams', fontsize=11)
    ax2.set_yticklabels(ax2.get_yticklabels(), fontsize=8)

    plt.suptitle('2023-24 NHL: Do All Three Generations Agree?', fontsize=13, y=1.01,
                 fontweight='bold')
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig('plots/11_cf_xgf_rank_comparison.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight', facecolor='#0d1117')
    plt.show()








Figure 7: CF% vs xGF% for the top 16 teams of 2023-24 (left), and a rank comparison across three metric generations (right). Where the columns agree, the signal is strong. Where they diverge — like teams ranked 8th in points but 18th in CF% — there’s an interesting analytical story to tell.





Boston and Carolina sit in the top-right quadrant — elite in both CF% and xGF%, meaning they dominate possession and shot quality. These are teams that analytics across all generations agree on.
The more interesting cases are the divergences. Teams ranked similarly in points but spread apart in Corsi vs xGF% are often being carried by a goalie with an unsustainably high save percentage (high PDO), or running a deliberate shot-blocking system that suppresses Corsi but not quality chances. Those are the situations where knowing which generation of metric to trust — and when — becomes the actual analytical skill.

title: A Brief History of NHL Analytics (2007–Present)
section: The Full Picture: A Timeline of Innovation
text:
The Full Picture: A Timeline of Innovation


Code
events = [
    (2007, 'Jim Corsi begins tracking\nall shot attempts in Buffalo', 1, 1),
    (2008, 'Vic Ferrari publishes\nCorsi/Fenwick on behindthenet.ca', 1, -1),
    (2010, 'Tyler Dellow: Corsi predicts\nfuture team success (r=0.72)', 1, 1),
    (2012, 'War on Ice launches\n(Macdonald & Thomas)', 1, -1),
    (2013, 'NaturalStatTrick debuts;\nCorsi goes mainstream', 1, 1),
    (2014, 'Puck Analytics: first NHL\nxG models published', 2, -1),
    (2015, 'Moneypuck.com launches\n(Peter Tanner xG model)', 2, 1),
    (2016, 'NHL Analytics Summit;\nteams hiring full-time quants', 2, -1),
    (2017, 'Evolving Hockey publishes\nGAR/WAR framework', 3, 1),
    (2018, 'Sznajder All Three Zones\nzone entry database complete', 4, -1),
    (2019, 'NHL–Sportradar puck\ntracking pilot (select arenas)', 4, 1),
    (2020, 'COVID season: teams accelerate\nanalytics hiring', 3, -1),
    (2021, 'Sportlogiq powers\nNHL Edge tracking rollout', 4, 1),
    (2022, 'NHL Edge IQ launches:\nspeed, distance, puck tracking', 4, -1),
    (2023, 'Transformer-based\nsequence xG models appear', 4, 1),
    (2024, 'All 32 teams have\nfull analytics departments', 4, -1),
    (2025, 'AI-driven off-puck\nvaluation enters public research', 4, 1),
]

gen_colors_map = {1: TEAL, 2: GREEN, 3: GOLD, 4: ORANGE}
gen_labels     = {1: 'Gen 1: Corsi/Fenwick', 2: 'Gen 2: xG / PDO',
                  3: 'Gen 3: WAR / RAPM',    4: 'Gen 4: Tracking'}

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(16, 7))
ax.set_facecolor('#0d1117')
fig.patch.set_facecolor('#0d1117')
ax.axhline(0, color='#30363d', lw=2, zorder=1)

for x0, x1, gen in [(2007,2014,1),(2014,2018,2),(2018,2022,3),(2022,2026,4)]:
    ax.axvspan(x0, x1, alpha=0.07, color=gen_colors_map[gen])
    ax.text((x0+x1)/2, -2.8, gen_labels[gen], ha='center', fontsize=8.5,
            color=gen_colors_map[gen], fontweight='bold')

for year, label, gen, offset in events:
    col   = gen_colors_map[gen]
    y_end = 1.5 * offset
    ax.plot([year, year], [0, y_end * 0.85], color=col, lw=1.2, zorder=2)
    ax.scatter(year, 0, color=col, s=60, zorder=3)
    ax.text(year, y_end, label, ha='center', va='bottom' if offset > 0 else 'top',
            fontsize=7, color='#e6edf3',
            bbox=dict(boxstyle='round,pad=0.25', fc='#161b22', ec=col, alpha=0.85, lw=0.8))

ax.set_xlim(2006.2, 2025.8)
ax.set_ylim(-3.5, 3.5)
ax.set_xticks(range(2007, 2026))
ax.set_xticklabels([str(y) for y in range(2007, 2026)], rotation=45, fontsize=8)
ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_title('NHL Analytics: A History of Innovation (2007–2025)',
             fontsize=14, pad=15, color='white', fontweight='bold')

patches_legend = [mpatches.Patch(color=v, label=gen_labels[k]) for k, v in gen_colors_map.items()]
ax.legend(handles=patches_legend, fontsize=8.5, loc='upper left',
          facecolor='#161b22', edgecolor='#30363d')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('plots/10_analytics_timeline.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight', facecolor='#0d1117')
plt.show()








Figure 8: The NHL analytics timeline from 2007 to 2025, organized by metric generation. Each wave of innovation was driven by a specific failure mode in the previous generation.

title: A Brief History of NHL Analytics (2007–Present)
section: Conclusion: Why No Single Metric Is Ever Enough
text:
Conclusion: Why No Single Metric Is Ever Enough
The history of NHL analytics is not a story of one metric replacing another. It’s a story of layering — each generation answering a question the previous one couldn’t ask.







The problem
The solution




+/− is too noisy
→ Corsi: higher sample rate, real signal


Corsi ignores shot quality
→ xG: probability-weighted shots


xG can’t isolate individual skill
→ WAR/RAPM: regression-based context adjustment


WAR can’t see off-puck play
→ Tracking: full player and puck data



The modern analyst doesn’t choose between these generations. They read all four simultaneously, the way a doctor synthesizes blood work, imaging, and clinical observation before making a diagnosis. Each layer confirms, challenges, or contextualizes the others.
Corsi isn’t enough. But it was the insight that started everything — and understanding why it isn’t enough is what makes every metric that came after it meaningful.
